Alright, you're back. Time to roll forward. Here's the deal. Before I start digging into the actual nuts and bolts of straight line prospecting, there's one particular business concept that I want to make sure you understand fully because if you don't, it's going to seriously undermine your ability to become a top producer in your field, not to mention the fact. It'll also keep you very confused for the rest of the training. So in light of that, it definitely makes sense for me to quickly address it here. Promise I will quickly. It's pretty basic stuff, but I just want to make sure that you have at least a basic understanding of it or else it'll undermine your ability to use the straight line at the highest level to become a top producer, which means making a ton of money and having an awesome life. So with that in mind, what I'm referring to here is the incestuous relationship between marketing and sales. So far as how these two completely separate yet totally interrelated functions work hand in hand with one another to allow a business to successfully monetize value, which in essence is what being in business is all about, monetizing value. In other words, you have something of value, product, good or service, and you form a business to essentially deliver that value either to other people or to other businesses. There's both B2B and B2C. And to do that in a cost effective manner, so at the end of the day, the company ends up making money. That's the purpose of a business. Make sense? Okay, great. So to that end, you have marketing on one side of the equation and you have sales on the other. Now, marketing is the process by which a company basically looks out into the world and analyzes the entire potential universe of prospective buyers with a goal of identifying the very best ones of all in terms of which are most likely to both want and need the product and also afford the product and then choose a certain marketing strategy that will allow them to reach out to these buyers with a sort of message, which is called a message to market match at industry ponds. In the sense that message matches the buyers needs directly, so you can ultimately bring these prospective buyers into the company's pipeline as the phrase goes, I know a lot of buzzwords here, I'll get to all of them on my one, but at which point we now move over to the sales side of the equation when you actually have these leads that come in and now they distribute them to the actual sales force who will then sift through them one by one, see which makes sense to actually give presentations to, because you're not going to present to everybody, so the sales force can actually close them. Make sense? It's pretty basic stuff here, but what tends to trip people up are all the new ones because there's lots and lots of them. So what I want to do here is sort out the important ones first, starting with what type of sales and marketing process your company is currently using. So we do some interactivity here, for example, you're going to choose one of these. In your business right now, while people showing up at a store, I use the term store very loosely here, that store could be any retail establishment, that could be a wholesale establishment, it could be a warehouse, a car dealership, insurance agency, real estate place, medical office, you get it. Basically any situation where prospective buyers are showing up at your place of business, that's one option. Option two, does your marketing system you call calling people on the phone or telemarketing as the phrase goes? Or third, are you knocking on doors out in the field? So your fourth option is your marketing being done online, where prospective buyers basically opt into a website, some sort of landing page, and then go through a marketing funnel, which results in you getting a qualified lead to call back at some point. Which one of these best fits your current situation? Take your time here, because I want you to really get this right because I'm going to change my response seriously based on your answer. Okay, great. So your first point of contact with your prospects is when they walk into your store as the phrase goes. So what basically happens here is that your company will have some type of marketing campaign, and by the way that could even be simply word of mouth, but usually there'll be some type of advertising going on, either offline or online, and there was some type of call to action in the end, which caused people to show up at your store to find out more about your product. You get it? But either way, what I really want you to understand here is that from a pure sales perspective, this is where your job actually begins as a salesperson. In that first moment when a prospect came through your door as a result of your company's marketing efforts. Now, just to be clear, in the real world of sales, people are going to be walking in your door for a host of different reasons. Now, it's not going to always be so cut and dry where every person who enters your store is going to come from your company's marketing campaign, and hence will be at least somewhat familiar with your product. You'd have to quickly suss that out many times yourself in terms of where they came from, what they actually know, and what their true level of interest is. See, my point here is no matter what any prospect's reason is for walking through your door, your job as a salesperson begins at the first moment when they cross over the threshold and you find out that they're actually interested in what you have. Before that, it's actually still marketing. You're sussing them out. But once they're actually interested, that's when marketing ends and you start qualifying them and that's where sales begins. Okay, awesome. You're cold calling on the phone or to put it formally, you're telemarketing or as I refer to it, you're dialing for dollars. Whatever the case, this obviously falls right into my sweet spot. I mean, I've created more million-dollar producing telemarketers than every other sales trade in the world combined and I feel pretty confident saying that. And I think you do too, right? You know the deal. Anyway, the key point I want you to remember here is there are different types of call systems in sales cycles as far as that. There are one call cycles, two call cycles, three call cycles, which I'll discuss in more detail later on. But the key thing to remember here is that the marketing process is where you're making those massive number of outgoing calls, going wide essentially, trying to find someone who's interested in actually speaking to you. And when you actually find that someone and they agree to start answering your prospecting questions and I'll get to what those questions are later in this module, that's the point where the marketing process ends and the sales process begins. In other words, marketing is the process of sifting through large numbers of people and sales is when you get a human being on the telephone who's actually interested. That's the difference. Okay, awesome. You're in door to door sales. Let me just tell you one thing. That is also one of my main specialties. I think I've actually done more door to door selling than telephone selling. I've done both at a very high level and I've always been the top producer in the world at this stuff. So here's the deal. It's very much like telemarketing in the sense of the distinction between marketing and sales. In the case of outside door to door sales, it's business sales, door to door home sales, the separation between marketing and sales that when you're out there knocking on massive numbers of doors, businesses or homes, that process is marketing. You're sifting through a large universe of potential buyers looking to find someone who's interested. That is always marketing. And then when you actually find someone who says, oh, really? Well, tell me more about that. You start asking them questions and they answer. Honestly, that's when the sales process begins. Okay, awesome. You're an online market, really sure company is. And this is also one of my specialties. Now, here's the deal. The best way to explain this to you is to tell you a really funny story. In fact, I'll actually show you footage of an event I did many years back and I said, had these guys rolling on the floor, not living, almost rolling. I was speaking at a Frank Curve, a very famous digital marketer, and I came up with this line on the top. I said, guys, you know, the problem with most digital marketers is they think that if they master the art of digital marketing, that they'll never have to speak to another human being as long as they live. And I said, that's ridiculous. And everyone started laughing and they started saying, yeah, you know, we're kind of like that. The misconception that some internet marketers have is they think that if they perfect the internet marketing side of things, they want to deal with other human beings on some level. It's just bullshit. You still got to communicate with people. The point is, is that I don't care how awesome you are at marketing online. There's somewhat of a cap of how much someone will send you a buy from you until they actually speak to a living, breathing human being in the form of a salesperson. You get it? So marketing online, I love it. I do it all the time. My business is based on that, right? But I know that I am limited to probably 99 or 990 at the very high end of what someone will feel comfortable sending in without having a salesperson call them and guide them through the process and tell them more. So I'm not going to get into internet marketing out. So it's a totally different thing. I just want you to understand the distinction between marketing and sales on the internet. So as an internet marketer, yeah, you have to perfect all those skills that allow you to bring in massive numbers of leads to your landing pages and so forth, your opt-ins, right? But if you want to really be one of the best of the best and really make money, well, guess what? You're going to have to go one step further. After you get them as customers or they opt-in, you got to have someone pick up the phone and call and explain exactly what the features and benefits are, what the value of proposition is and also create urgency and boom, use the straight license to close the highest level. You do that, the world is yours. Now, for all of you, no matter which of those processes you chose, one point I always want you to remember because it's ain't changing and ain't going away is that there are actually two types of marketing nowadays, right? You have all-fly marketing, which is your traditional TV, radio, newspaper ads, your door-knockin' campaigns, tele-marketing, basically your old-fashioned marketing, right? And now you also have your online marketing, which is your Facebook, your Google, YouTube ads, social media, SEO, search engine optimization to get your link up to the top of Google so people click on it organically, right? So you have online marketing and you have all-fly marketing. Two very different types of processes, but here's the deal. They both have the same end goal in mind, which is to bring in as many qualified prospects as possible into your sales funnel so you can now call them and turn them into dollars. Now, just one more point here with marketing before we move back into the core of straight-line prospecting and that is what to expect from the marketing department that gives you leads or if you have to do this yourself here, at least just to know the basics here so you're not flying totally blind. This is not meant to be marketing train that you could actually use. It's just the basic distinctions, food for thought. Step number one in any marketing campaign is always about identifying who your best buyers are. That's always step one. Moving on then to step number two, you have to then bring those buyers into your sales funnel in a cost-effective manner. In other words, it's not going to work out very well for you or your company if at the end of the day, of course, you modify the buyer and bring them to your door, then you actually make when you sell them something. Now, that being said, remember we have the life value of the customer. So it's not always about what you make on the first sale, but over the life of the relationship. The point is that there's a value exchange, meaning that what you sell this person over the long term better be worth more than a cost to get them into your funnel. Pretty obvious stuff, right? With that number three, this has to do with the actual message to market. Remember I said that you have to have a good message to market match. Make sure you spend the time to really understand what your customers core needs are and you want to speak to those needs by having a solid message to market match. That has what's called a hook at the end. So that's sexy and kind of grabs their attention and also if possible, you want to add some urgency in there, which dramatically gets people to essentially click now because they might run out of whatever it is you're selling. That's what urgency does. Okay. Now, lastly, number four here, this has to do with the seamless transition of leads going from the marketing department to sales. And by the way, this is where companies fall down miserably. I'll tell you a story. I actually did a trade for a company. They were spending massive amounts of money on internet marketing and saying, why are we always short of leads? Well, guess what? Turned out they had a computerized dialing system and what the system was doing cause a lead was being mistagged every time it got a no answer once. So as soon as it got one no answer into the dead file, 75% of the people were never being dialed more than once. And when that got fixed, it was unbelievable. And it was a win for a point being track your leads carefully. If you're on the marketing side of the equation, you be very, very careful that the lead gets tracked the entire way from the moment that they get contact coming to your company to the moment it gets actually handed off to the salesperson. It's got to be a seamless exchange the whole way through. You get it? And those are your four basic, very basic points on marketing that you know, just to not simply get destroyed and also just to not sound like an idiot when people speak to you. Just know the link. All right. Now just one final point here before we move on to your next lesson, which is that when it comes to the real world effectiveness of any marketing campaign, when you're out there in the field, what you'll find is that no matter how targeted your company's campaign was, no matter how much care they put in to try to get only the right types of buyers to enter the funnel, meaning people who need your product, want your product, and can actually afford your product. At the end of the day, only a fraction of these people who enter your funnel, the wide part, maybe 50, 60% at most will have even a shot of buying your product, meaning they want it, need it, and can actually afford it. The rest of them will have to be removed from the equation as quickly and elegantly as possible. Now to that end, with the straight line, we have a very specific nomenclature that we use to divide these buyers up into these four categories. And when I see you in the next lesson, we'll go through that exact thing. But for right now, I want you to take a quiz. There's a lot of stuff we went through here. Just make sure you have it locked down tight. And when you're done, meet me in the next lesson. I will see you there.
